topic.prompt_en: Compare the past and present in an area of life you know.
topic.learning_goal: Learn to compare past and present through clear contrast and a realistic judgment.

angles.title: State your position
content: Answer the question directly at the start.
A clear position gives the rest of the answer direction.

angles.title: Give the main reason
content: Choose one or two reasons that carry the answer.
Do not pile up weak points just to sound complete.

angles.title: Acknowledge the other side
content: Show what the alternative can offer.
This makes the answer sound more mature and realistic.

angles.title: End with a balanced conclusion
content: Finish with the condition, limit, or context that matters.
That keeps the answer flexible rather than extreme.

sequence.target: State your main view, preference, or comparison clearly.
Support your point and mention the alternative side.
End with the context where your view may change.

high_score_tips: A balanced opinion sounds stronger than a dramatic or rigid statement.
Give one concrete example if the topic risks becoming too abstract.
Use flexible language like generally, in my experience, or in some cases.
Your closing sentence should show when your opinion applies and when it might not.

content.problem: Taking an extreme position too quickly
content.why_it_hurts: The answer sounds less thoughtful if there is no room for context or exceptions.
content.fix: Keep your main view clear, but acknowledge the conditions around it.

content.why_it_hurts: A comparison is weak if the listener does not know what standard you are using.
content.fix: Compare cost, time, comfort, freedom, or long-term effect explicitly.

content.why_it_hurts: Some topics need nuance, and a fully certain answer can sound unrealistic.
content.fix: Use phrases like to some extent, in most cases, or it depends on.

language.wrong_example: I am agree with this idea.
language.reason: Agree is a verb, so it does not take am.

language.wrong_example: This option is more better for me.
language.better_example: This option is better for me.
language.reason: Better already carries the comparative meaning.

core.example: I would naturally use "viewpoint" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "comparison" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "difference" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "condition" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "reason" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "context" when talking about this topic.

extended.note: Useful when your answer depends on conditions.
Useful when you want to avoid sounding rigid.

core.example: I would use "from my point of view" naturally in this answer.
I would use "it depends on" naturally in this answer.
I would use "on the one hand" naturally in this answer.
I would use "on the other hand" naturally in this answer.
I would use "I partly agree because" naturally in this answer.
I would use "the main difference is that" naturally in this answer.

extended.phrase: I would not make it absolute
extended.meaning_zh: 我不会把它说成绝对的
extended.example: I would use "I would not make it absolute" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: it works better in some cases than others
extended.meaning_zh: 它在某些情况下更有效
extended.example: I would use "it works better in some cases than others" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: the stronger point for me is
extended.meaning_zh: 对我来说更有力的一点是
extended.example: I would use "the stronger point for me is" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: I can still see the other side
extended.example: I would use "I can still see the other side" when making the answer sound more natural.

framework.goal: Express a view through position, reason, contrast, and condition.

moves: State your view or preference directly.
Add one quick sentence to frame the topic.
Explain your main reason and compare it with the other side.
Use one clear criterion to keep the answer organised.
End with a condition, limit, or flexible conclusion.
Avoid sounding absolute if the topic is context-dependent.

delivery_markers: That said, I can see why ...

samples.answer:
A comparison between how something used to be and how it feels now is something I can talk about quite naturally. If I compare how people communicate now with how they communicated ten years ago, the biggest difference is speed. Messages are faster and more convenient now, which is useful in daily life, but that speed can also make conversations feel thinner or easier to misunderstand. In the past, slower communication often meant people wrote or spoke with a bit more intention, even if it was less efficient. I would not say the past was automatically better, but I do think we have traded some depth for convenience.

What made it worth talking about was not only the event or idea itself, but also the effect it had on the way I think or act. So for me, the comparison is not about nostalgia but about understanding what each period gives us. Overall, it is a topic that feels real to me because it connects to ordinary life rather than a perfect story.
highlights: Band 6 answer matched to the 比较过去与现在 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

opening: The topic that comes to mind is a comparison between how something used to be and how it feels now.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: It mattered because So for me, the comparison is not about nostalgia but about understanding what each period gives us.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: In the past, slower communication often meant people wrote or spoke with a bit more intention, even if it was less efficient.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

samples.answer:
The topic that comes to mind for me is a comparison between how something used to be and how it feels now. If I compare how people communicate now with how they communicated ten years ago, the biggest difference is speed. Messages are faster and more convenient now, which is useful in daily life, but that speed can also make conversations feel thinner or easier to misunderstand. What stood out most was that in the past, slower communication often meant people wrote or spoke with a bit more intention, even if it was less efficient.

After that, i would not say the past was automatically better, but I do think we have traded some depth for convenience. What I find interesting about it now is that the situation seemed fairly ordinary at the time, but it revealed something more lasting about how I handle pressure, choices, or relationships. So for me, the comparison is not about nostalgia but about understanding what each period gives us. So if I were explaining this in a speaking test, I would focus less on sounding dramatic and more on why the experience or idea genuinely stayed with me.

samples.answer:
If I had to answer this topic in a more reflective way, I would still begin with a comparison between how something used to be and how it feels now. If I compare how people communicate now with how they communicated ten years ago, the biggest difference is speed. Messages are faster and more convenient now, which is useful in daily life, but that speed can also make conversations feel thinner or easier to misunderstand. The turning point, for me, was that in the past, slower communication often meant people wrote or spoke with a bit more intention, even if it was less efficient.

From there, i would not say the past was automatically better, but I do think we have traded some depth for convenience. What matters now is not the surface event or statement alone, but the shift in perspective behind it. So for me, the comparison is not about nostalgia but about understanding what each period gives us. In that sense, the topic feels useful because it shows not only what happened or what I think, but also how my judgment has become a little more precise over time.

practice.task: Compare the past and present in an area of life you know.
checklist: Compare with at least one clear criterion.
Use a flexible ending if the topic depends on context.
self_prompts: What can the other side offer?
